questions.question: Explain the differences between ifstream, ofstream, and fstream classes in C++.
keyPoints: ifstream is for reading (input) from files - derived from istream
ofstream is for writing (output) to files - derived from ostream
fstream provides both reading and writing capabilities - derived from iostream
ifstream opens file in ios::in mode by default
ofstream opens file in ios::out | ios::trunc mode by default
fstream requires explicit mode specification

questions.question: What are the various file opening modes in C++? Explain any four with their purposes.
keyPoints: ios::in - Open for reading (input)
ios::out - Open for writing (output)
ios::app - Append to end of file
ios::trunc - Truncate existing file (default for ofstream)
ios::binary - Open in binary mode (no translation)
ios::ate - Position at end initially but allows random access
ios::nocreate - Open only if file exists
ios::noreplace - Open only if file doesn't exist

questions.question: Explain the purpose and usage of seekg() and tellg() functions in file handling.
keyPoints: seekg() positions the get (read) pointer in a file
tellg() returns the current position of the get pointer
Both are used for random access file reading
seekg(offset, reference) allows positioning relative to beginning/current/end
tellg() returns streampos type representing byte offset
Used to navigate through file contents non-sequentially

questions.question: Differentiate between text mode and binary mode file operations in C++.
keyPoints: Text mode translates newline characters (\n to \r\n on Windows)
Binary mode reads/writes exact byte representation
Binary mode is faster as no translation occurs
Text mode may cause issues with binary data (e.g., images)
Binary mode uses read()/write() member functions
Text mode uses << and >> operators or get()/put()

questions.question: Write a C++ program to copy contents of one file to another using file streams.
keyPoints: Include <fstream> header
Open source file in input mode (ios::in)
Open destination file in output mode (ios::out)
Read from source using >> or get()
Write to destination using << or put()
Check file opened successfully using is_open()
Close both files

questions.question: Explain the ios::app and ios::ate modes. How do they differ?
keyPoints: ios::app (append) - Opens file and positions pointer at end, all writes go to end only
ios::ate (at end) - Opens file and positions pointer at end initially but allows seeking
ios::app maintains end position for every write operation
ios::ate allows random access anywhere in the file
Use ios::app for log files, ios::ate for updating specific records

questions.question: What is the purpose of the is_open() function? How do you check if a file opened successfully?
keyPoints: is_open() checks if file stream is connected to an open file
Returns boolean - true if successful, false if failed
Also can check stream object in boolean context
Useful for error handling before file operations
Example: if(!outfile.is_open()) { cerr << "Error"; }
Alternative: if(outfile) { // file opened }

questions.question: Write a C++ program to perform the following operations on a file: (a) Create a file and write student data (name, roll number, marks) (b) Display the data (c) Search for a student by roll number (d) Delete a student record.
keyPoints: Define Student class with name, rollno, marks
Function to create file and write records using ofstream
Function to read and display all records using ifstream
Function to search by roll number - read sequentially and compare
Function to delete record - create temp file, skip record to delete, copy rest
Use binary mode for efficient record operations
Proper error handling and file closure
Menu-driven interface for user options

questions.question: Explain random access file handling in C++ using seekp(), seekp(), tellg(), and tellp(). Write a program to update a specific record in a file.
keyPoints: seekg(pos) - move get pointer to absolute position
seekg(offset, dir) - move with offset from beginning/current/end
tellg() - returns current read position as streampos
tellp() - returns current write position as streampos
streampos type stores byte offset from beginning
For record update: calculate position = (recordno-1) * sizeof(record)
Use seekp() to position at record start
Use write() to overwrite the record
Always update in binary mode for fixed-size records

questions.question: Write a C++ program to maintain an inventory file with the following fields: Item code, Item name, Quantity, Price. Implement options to: (i) Add new items (ii) Display all items (iii) Update quantity (iv) Display items with quantity below threshold.
keyPoints: Define Item structure with required fields
Function to add items - open in append mode, write new records
Function to display all - read and print all records
Function to update quantity - use seekp() to position at record
Display low stock - read all, check quantity < threshold
Use binary mode for efficient storage
Menu-driven main function
Error handling for file operations
Proper use of ios::binary and ios::app modes

questions.question: Explain the difference between sequential access and random access file handling. How does C++ support both?
keyPoints: Sequential access - read/write records one after another from beginning
Random access - directly jump to any record without reading previous ones
C++ supports sequential through normal >> and << operations
Random access supported by seekg(), seekp(), tellg(), tellp()
seekg(offset, reference) allows positioning: ios::beg, ios::cur, ios::end
Random access efficient for large files - no need to skip records
Binary files with fixed-size records enable true random access
Example: to access 5th record in binary file: seekp(4*sizeof(record), ios::beg)
